1: The consultation of all the commanders including Johanan son of Kareah and all the people from the least to the greatest — a comprehensive assembly — gives the request for prophetic guidance maximum social weight. The gathering mirrors the covenant assembly format: all strata of society present to receive YHWH's word together. The inclusive formula ensures that no one can later claim they were not represented in the decision to seek and hear the oracle.
2: The petition — let our plea for mercy fall before you (tippol naʾ teḥinātenu lefanekā) — uses the formulaic language of court petition and lament prayer. The acknowledgment that we are but a few out of the many frames the community's fragility: a remnant that has survived destruction, deportation, and assassination, now dwindling and desperate. The request to pray to YHWH your God — the possessive "your" God rather than "our" — may reflect either deference to the prophet's special relationship with YHWH or a subtle distancing from the divine.
3: The specific request — that YHWH your God may tell us the way we should go and the thing we should do — frames the inquiry as a request for divine guidance on a decision already under consideration. The question of where to go (Egypt or stay) and what to do (flee or remain) was already being debated; they want prophetic confirmation. The irony developed in subsequent verses is that they had already made up their minds — the consultation was performative rather than genuinely open-ended.
4: Jeremiah's acceptance of the prophetic commission — I have heard you, I will pray to YHWH your God according to your words and whatever YHWH answers you I will tell you — commits him to full transparency: he will report whatever comes, whether convenient or not. The phrase lo ʾekhasseh mimmekkem davar (I will not withhold a word from you) is a prophetic integrity formula, promising complete disclosure. The repetition of "your God" here is notable.
5: The oath the community swears — may YHWH be a true and faithful witness against us if we do not act according to all the word that YHWH your God sends you to us — is a self-imprecatory oath with YHWH as the enforcement party. Invoking YHWH as witness against oneself in case of non-compliance is the strongest possible binding form. The oath structure ensures they cannot later claim the oracle was unclear or that compliance was optional. It becomes the legal basis for Jeremiah's later accusation.
6: The explicit commitment — whether it is good or bad, we will obey the voice of YHWH our God — anticipates the possibility of an unwelcome oracle and commits in advance to obedience. The phrase ṭov ʾim raʿ (good or bad) covers both possibilities. This sweeping advance commitment makes subsequent disobedience all the more culpable: they cannot claim surprise at an unfavorable oracle or that they only agreed to comply if the answer was what they wanted.
7: The ten days that passed before the word came is a significant detail: prophetic inquiry was not instantaneous. The waiting period reflects the authentic practice of prophetic consultation — the prophet must wait, pray, and receive the word in its own time. The ten-day interval may have been deliberately noted to distinguish genuine prophecy (which requires waiting) from the kind of ready-made, socially convenient prophecy that the false prophets had provided.
8: Jeremiah's summoning of all the commanders and all the people from the least to the greatest for the delivery of the oracle recreates the assembly of Jeremiah 42:1 — the same comprehensive gathering that petitioned him now receives the answer. The symmetry is deliberate: the word is delivered to the same constituency that requested it, with the same formula of comprehensive inclusion. The oracle will be publicly and formally delivered to the entire community.
9: The delivery formula — thus says YHWH God of Israel to whom you sent me to present your plea before him — formally identifies the oracle's source and traces the consultative chain: the community petitioned Jeremiah, Jeremiah petitioned YHWH, YHWH now answers. The protocol is transparent and the authority chain explicit. The identification of YHWH as "God of Israel" invokes the covenant relationship as the frame for the oracle that follows.
10: The core command — if you will remain in this land, I will build you up and not pull you down — uses the characteristic Jeremian vocabulary of building/planting and pulling down/uprooting that frames the entire book (Jeremiah 1:10). The promise to plant you and not uproot you reverses the judgment language of the prophetic commission. Staying in the land activates covenant blessing; the word for "relent" (niḥamti) acknowledges that YHWH has been moved by the disaster already inflicted on the community.
11: The reassurance — do not fear the king of Babylon — directly addresses the stated rationale for the Egyptian flight (Jeremiah 41:17-18). The Babylonian reprisal the community feared is addressed by divine promise: YHWH will be with them, deliver them, and show them compassion. The phrase ʾani ʾitkem (I am with you) is the covenant presence formula that runs from the patriarchs through the Exodus to the prophets — the divine name Emmanuel (God-with-us) in covenant language.
12: YHWH's promise to show compassion (raḥamim) and cause the Babylonian king to show compassion — making him allow them to return to their land — frames Babylonian policy as ultimately under divine control. The diplomatic and military realism of Babylonian imperial administration is not ignored; rather, YHWH's control over the king's disposition is the claimed basis for the community's safety. The covenant God who controls international affairs can secure the remnant's position.
13: The conditional reversal — but if you say, we will not remain in this land, disobeying YHWH's voice — frames the Egyptian option as covenant disobedience. The choice between land and Egypt is not merely practical but theological: Egypt represents the reversal of the Exodus, a return to the "house of slavery" that YHWH had liberated Israel from. Choosing Egypt over the land YHWH promised and commanded them to inhabit is an act of covenant faithlessness.
14: The stated reasons for choosing Egypt — we will go to the land of Egypt where we will not see war or hear the trumpet or hunger for bread — reflect genuine human fears: the community had experienced traumatic warfare, had heard the siege signals, had endured famine conditions. The desire for a stable, peaceful environment is understandable. Egypt in this period was prosperous, politically organized, and not under immediate Babylonian threat. The appeal of Egypt was real, not irrational.
15: The counter-oracle — hear the word of YHWH, O remnant of Judah — addresses the community by the title that signals their reduced status: they are the "remnant" (sheʾerit), all that remains of a once-great people. The repeated oracle formula signals a new declaration: if you have set your faces to go to Egypt and go, then hear what YHWH says. The divine word does not override human freedom but announces consequences.
16: The reversal — the sword you fear will overtake you there in the land of Egypt — is the central irony of the oracle. Egypt, chosen as a refuge from the sword, will prove no refuge at all. The sword, famine, and plague that had pursued Israel in Judah will follow them to Egypt. The three classic covenant curses (sword, famine, plague) that Jeremiah has repeatedly invoked throughout the book are now attached to the Egyptian flight rather than the Judean land.
17: The comprehensive scope — all the people who set their faces to go to Egypt to live there will die by sword, famine, and plague; none will survive or escape the disaster I will bring upon them — leaves no exceptions. The phrasing echoes the siege oracles against Jerusalem, but now applied to Egypt. The covenant curses are not territorially bounded; they follow the disobedient community wherever it goes. YHWH's judgment is not avoidable by geography.
18: The comparison to how my wrath was poured out on the inhabitants of Jerusalem grounds the Egypt oracle in the already-fulfilled judgment. The community had witnessed the destruction of Jerusalem; the oracle says: that same divine wrath, poured out on the city you watched burn, will fall on you in Egypt. The comparison makes the threat concrete by referencing something they had already experienced. Alah (oath/curse) and sheriqah (horror/hissing) are the standard terms for covenant curse fulfillment.
19: The direct accusation — you have gone astray at the cost of your lives; you yourselves sent me to YHWH your God saying, pray for us — invokes the oath of Jeremiah 42:5-6 against the community. Having sworn to obey whatever word came, their evident decision to go to Egypt anyway constitutes perjury against YHWH. The word taʿitem (you have gone astray/erred) echoes the language of wandering from the path, the standard metaphor for covenant infidelity.
20: The formal charge — know for certain that you will die by sword, famine, and plague in the place where you want to go and live — completes the oracle. The repeated knowledge formula (yadeaʿ tedaʿu, know for certain) is judicial: it functions as formal notification that the community has been warned and cannot later claim ignorance. Having been warned, they bear full responsibility for their choice. The prophetic witness has been given; the community's fate is now self-determined.
21: Jeremiah's statement — I have told you today but you have not obeyed the voice of YHWH your God in anything he sent me to tell you — uses the same formula he has applied to all Israel's historical covenant failures. The word hayyom (today) marks this as the moment of formal prophetic declaration: the oracle has been given, the warning issued, the choice clarified. Today is always the moment of decision in Deuteronomic theology (cf. Deuteronomy 30:15-19).
22: The closing certainty formula — now therefore know for certain that you will die by sword, famine, and plague in the place where you want to go to live — repeats the consequence oracle one final time for emphasis. The triple repetition of the consequence (Jeremiah 42:16, 17, 22) employs the rhetorical principle that important legal or prophetic pronouncements are triply stated for emphasis and permanence. The oracle is now on the record; Jeremiah has fulfilled his prophetic responsibility regardless of what the community chooses.
